Reproduction study · SWIR hyperspectral · Cuvis.AI framework

Reproducing a hyperspectral steel-scrap classifier with Cuvis.AI

Gursch et al. (2026) classify single SWIR hyperspectral pixels into 14 steel-scrap materials with three neural models and an object-level majority vote, reaching 76.5% pixel accuracy with their best model. We set out to rebuild the whole pipeline on the Cuvis.AI framework — its own nodes and trainers rather than a one-off script — and see how close a faithful reproduction lands.

170 SWIR cubes (437 bands) + class masks · StatisticalTrainer → GradientTrainer · 10 of 14 classes present in this data release

How it went. All three models and the object-level vote rebuilt cleanly as a Cuvis.AI pipeline. The 3D-CNN is the strongest: it matches the paper's pixel accuracy on a whole-frame split (73.6% vs 76.5%) and reaches 62.9% under the paper's stricter leak-safe per-piece split; the object-level vote lifts dense accuracy 79.4% → 86.4%. The paper's model ranking (3D-CNN > 2D-CNN > MLP) reproduces.

The paper

The task is per-pixel material classification of scrap-steel piles imaged with a SWIR hyperspectral camera (437 bands). Three classifiers work on 7×7 spatial-spectral patches — an MLP on the centre spectrum, a 2D-CNN, and a 3D-CNN — trained with a class-weighted cross-entropy loss. At inference the per-pixel predictions are pooled into an object-level majority vote. The headline result is the 3D-CNN at 76.5% pixel accuracy (Table 2); copper is the paper's weak point, confused with steel.

How we rebuilt it in Cuvis.AI

Everything runs as a Cuvis.AI pipeline of framework nodes: a per-band standardizer feeds the classifier, whose logits drive a weighted cross-entropy loss node and a multiclass segmentation-metrics node. Training is two-phase — the StatisticalTrainer fits the global per-band mean/std, then the GradientTrainer (Lightning underneath) optimises the classifier with Adam. The object-level vote is a node that runs connected-components per frame and takes the majority class inside each blob. No custom training loop: the same trainers and node contracts that ship with the framework do the work.

Real frames, real predictions

modelpx accmacro Pmacro Rpx accmacro Pmacro R

2D-CNN62.467.1 69.9 74.183.068.0
3D-CNN73.673.5 79.5 76.583.669.6

Does the train/test split matter?

It matters a lot. The paper splits individual pieces 80/20, so no piece's pixels cross train and test. A simpler whole-frame split lets pixels from one physical scene land on both sides — optimistic. We ran both protocols.

Whole-frame 80/20 vs leak-safe per-piece 80/20 (paper protocol). px / macro-P / macro-R. Each column is that split's own test set, so this compares protocols rather than an identical-set delta.
modelframe splitobject split (paper)Δ px acc
MLP62.3 / 66.6 / 68.2 51.9 / 50.0 / 55.1−10.4
2D-CNN62.3 / 67.1 / 69.9 59.9 / 61.1 / 64.0−2.4
3D-CNN73.6 / 73.5 / 79.5 62.9 / 60.0 / 66.2−10.7

The object-level vote

A second stage: cleaning up the masks

The raw per-pixel prediction is spatially noisy — salt-and-pepper speckle inside otherwise solid pieces. As a separate post-processing stage we ran it through an off-the-shelf cleanup node from the Cuvis.AI node catalog (loaded from a local checkout as a plugin), with no retraining: MaskRobustifier applies per-class morphological opening/closing and drops small components, then fills the gaps from the nearest surviving label — turning the speckled map into solid regions.

Cleanup of the 3D-CNN prediction over the full test set. px / macro-P / macro-R, and the change in pixel accuracy vs the raw prediction.
stagepx accmacro Pmacro RΔ px acc
raw 3D-CNN prediction79.473.1 80.2—
MaskRobustifier (per-class morphology)83.577.7 84.1+4.1

How faithful is it to the paper?

The paper has no appendix; the architectures are fully specified in its §Method (p.4). We matched them closely and note the deliberate deviations:

  • MLP and 2D-CNN: exact — B→256→128→O, and 3× Conv2D-BN-ReLU 32/64/128 → global average pool → FC.
  • 3D-CNN: backbone exact (Conv3D 8/16/32, kernels 7/5/3 → Conv2D 64 → FC 256/128/O), with two additions — BatchNorm (the paper specifies it only for the 2D-CNN) and a MaxPool / AdaptiveAvgPool spectral collapse where the paper only says “reshaped”.
  • Loss weighting differs: the paper weights proportional to class frequency (w_o = n_o/Σn, eq. 1); we used inverse frequency, which favours minority classes and likely explains our higher-than-paper macro recall.
  • Early stopping (val-loss, patience 25) vs the paper's fixed 200 epochs. All three plateaued well before 200 (3D-CNN best at epoch 42), so this cost no accuracy.

How it went

A faithful, framework-native reproduction lands where the paper does: the 3D-CNN is the best model, tracks the paper's pixel accuracy on a whole-frame split, and the object-level vote behaves as described. Under the paper's stricter per-piece split the trustworthy pixel accuracy is 62.9%, and the model ranking is preserved throughout. A second, off-the-shelf cleanup stage from the Cuvis.AI node catalog (MaskRobustifier) then lifts the full-test prediction another 4 points (79.4% → 83.5%) with no retraining. The remaining gaps are about the data and a few deliberate choices, not the framework:

  • Copper is unanalysable here — zero labelled pixels in this release (only 10 of the paper's 14 classes appear), so the paper's signature copper/steel confusion cannot be reproduced.
  • The object vote is a connected-component proxy, not true piece instances; it over-merges adjacent pieces. The overall lift is real; the per-class collapse on minority materials is the proxy's limit.
  • Open Joanneum items: true piece-level instance maps, and the original per-pixel class IDs to finalise the provisional 29→14 colour merge.
Reproduction on the Cuvis.AI framework.
Paper: Gursch, Ofner, Harb, Jaschik, Ganster, Rieger, “Hyperspectral scrap characterisation for scrap composition optimisation in steel recycling”, Waste Management & Research 2026 (DOI 10.1177/0734242X261451604, open access).
Dataset: InSpecScrap HSI metal-scrap (Jaschik, Jernej), Zenodo DOI 10.5281/zenodo.17076238.
Method and data by the InSpecScrap consortium (Know-Center, TU Graz, JOANNEUM RESEARCH, K1-MET). Reproduction code licensed under Apache-2.0.
